hook: A coffee shop built almost entirely from salvaged junk, down a motorbike-hushed lane.
facts: Reached down 3B Hang Tre alley, signed with a note asking riders to push bikes in with engines off
Owner claims around 95 percent of furniture, cups and decor came from scrap and recycling
Spread over several cluttered floors of birdcages, old radios and bottle-lid mosaics
w: Down an alley signed with a plea to push your motorbike in engine-off, a coffee shop assembled almost wholly from other people's rubbish. Old radios, birdcages, typewriters, walls studded with bottle caps, the owner reckons nine in ten objects were salvaged. Junk, reborn as a den.

hook: The cramped alley cafe where egg coffee was invented in the lean years of 1946.
facts: Founder Nguyen Van Giang devised ca phe trung in 1946 when wartime milk ran scarce
Whisked egg yolk and condensed milk poured over strong coffee, served in a bowl of warm water
Still family-run, tucked at the end of a narrow passage off 39 Nguyen Huu Huan
w: When milk grew scarce in 1946, a bartender whisked egg yolk into a froth instead, and egg coffee was born here, at the back of a slot of an alley. The family still keeps the recipe, the bowl of warm water under the glass, the low stools. Order it hot and pretend the war never lifted.

hook: The tail and undercarriage of a downed bomber still jut from a pond between houses.
facts: B-52 shot down on 27 December 1972 during the Christmas bombing raids, falling into the lake
Remaining wreckage roughly eight metres long, including a twin tail fin, still breaking the water
Declared a national historical monument in 1992; locals keep the surrounding pond
w: Between cramped Ngoc Ha houses sits a small green pond, and from it juts the broken tail of an American bomber, downed in the Christmas raids of December 1972. Mossy, rusting, half-sunk, with washing lines strung nearby. A war trophy nobody bothered to drain away.

hook: A skeletal French iron bridge, bombed and patched, that still carries trains over the Red River.
facts: Built 1899 to 1902 by Paris firm Dayde and Pille, opened as the Paul Doumer Bridge
Heavily bombed in the American war and repaired piecemeal, leaving mismatched spans
Still carries a central rail line, with pedestrians and bikes on the flanks
w: Often miscredited to Eiffel, this iron spider was actually thrown across the Red River by Dayde and Pille around 1902. Bombs gnawed it in the war; the patched-back spans no longer match. Walk it at dusk among scooters and freight trains. Faded colonial muscle, still working.

hook: A whole lane of traditional medicine shops where the air carries roots, bark and dried herbs.
facts: Named for 18th-century physician Hai Thuong Lan Ong, Le Huu Trac
Said to hold the great majority of the district's oriental medicine shops
First traders came from Fujian, China; sacks of herbs still spill onto the pavement
w: Named for an 18th-century physician, this lane has sold roots, bark and dried beetles for centuries, the trade carried in by Fujianese settlers. Sacks of cinnamon and ginseng slump onto the pavement and the whole street smells faintly medicinal. Bring an ailment, leave with a paper twist.

hook: The lane of paper offerings, where you can buy a cardboard motorbike to burn for the dead.
facts: Specialises in votive paper goods burned so ancestors can use them in the afterlife
Paper effigies range from cars and phones to whole houses and bundles of hell money
Trade in paper goods on the street is said to go back centuries
legend: They say burning the paper goods sends the real thing through to relatives in the next world
w: A street that sells the afterlife by the armful: paper motorbikes, paper iPhones, paper villas, stacks of hell banknotes, all made to be burned so the dead can spend them. They say the smoke carries the real thing across. Lurid, festive, faintly unnerving. Shopping for ghosts.

hook: Hanoi's oldest temple, dim with incense, founded on a legend of a guiding white horse.
facts: Traditionally founded in the 11th century, the eastern guardian of old Thang Long
Much of the present woodwork dates from an 18th-century rebuilding
Holds a carved white horse and a red funerary palanquin among the smoke
legend: Legend says a white horse appeared and traced the line where Ly Thai To's city walls should stand
w: The oldest shrine in the city hides behind a red gate on a busy lane, its rafters black with a thousand years of incense. Legend says a white horse appeared to the founding king and walked the line where his walls should rise. Inside, a carved horse keeps watch. Old Hanoi distilled.

hook: A restored tube house showing how Old Quarter merchants squeezed a life into a sliver of street.
facts: Late 19th-century merchant home, once a shop selling medicinal herbs
Long, narrow tube-house plan with courtyards, born of taxes on street frontage
Restored with the French city of Toulouse and opened to the public in 1999
w: A merchant's tube house, narrow as a shoebox at the street and running deep behind, threaded with little courtyards to let in light and air. Once a herb shop, later crammed with five families, now restored. The shape came of a dodge: tax fell on your street frontage, so you built backwards instead.

hook: A residential rail corridor where cafes pull in their chairs minutes before a train scrapes past.
facts: Active line laid by the French around 1902, running between house fronts a hand's width from doors
Cafes set stools on the tracks and clear them as each scheduled train approaches
Closed in 2019 over safety, reopened with cafe-gated access, tours later restricted
w: A working French-laid railway runs a forearm's width from the front doors, and the residents long ago turned the verge into cafes. Stools go onto the rails; minutes before each train someone hauls them in, the carriages scrape past, then the coffee resumes. Semi-famous now, still genuinely daft.

hook: A French colonial prison, later the wry-named Hanoi Hilton, kept as a fragment of museum.
facts: Built by the French for Vietnamese political prisoners, with an original guillotine on display
Held captured American airmen in the war, who nicknamed it the Hanoi Hilton
Mostly demolished in the 1990s; the surviving gatehouse block became a museum
w: The French built it to break Vietnamese revolutionaries, and left behind a four-metre guillotine that still stands in its room. American pilots later sweated here and christened it the Hanoi Hilton. Most was torn down for an office tower; this surviving block holds the shackles, the cells, the dark.

hook: The single surviving gate of the old earthen city, a squat watchtower swallowed by traffic.
facts: Built in 1749, the last of many gates that once pierced the ramparts of Thang Long
Named for a commander said to have died defending it against the French
Restored with help from a US cultural preservation fund around 2010
w: Of all the gates that once studded the old earth ramparts, only this 1749 stub survives, a brick watchtower now marooned in scooter traffic. It takes its name from a commander said to have fallen here fighting the French. Stand under the arch and the whole vanished wall briefly returns.

hook: Kilometres of dyke wall tiled into a record-breaking ceramic mosaic for the city's millennium.
facts: Around four kilometres long, holding a Guinness record as the longest ceramic mosaic
Made for the 1,000th anniversary of Thang Long, completed in 2010
Tiles fired at nearby Bat Trang pottery village; designs span Vietnam's dynasties
w: Someone won a competition by proposing to tile the grey flood-dyke, and the result runs kilometres, a record-holding ribbon of mosaic made for the city's thousandth birthday. Bat Trang tiles march through dynasties, dragons and children's drawings. Best seen on foot, traffic roaring at your shoulder.

hook: A rooftop coffee hideout reached by walking through a silk shop and up a dim spiral stair.
facts: Entered through a souvenir and silk shop off Hang Gai, then a courtyard and narrow stairs
A family altar sits mid-climb before the steps reach the terrace
Top floor looks over the rooftops to Hoan Kiem Lake
w: To find the view you walk straight through a silk shop, across a hidden courtyard hung with birdcages, past a family altar, then up a tight spiral. The reward: a cluttered rooftop over the tiled roofs and Hoan Kiem Lake. Order the egg coffee. A secret hiding in plain sight.

hook: A Taoist temple guarding the north, built around a near-four-metre black bronze god.
facts: Dedicated to Tran Vu, Taoist guardian of the north, one of the city's four sacred temples
Holds a black-bronze statue from 1677, nearly four metres tall, cast by Ngu Xa artisans
Pilgrims rub a bronze foot smooth for luck
w: Northern guardian of the four temples that once warded the city, it broods around a black bronze deity cast in 1677, almost four metres of him, serpent and turtle at his feet. Generations of hopeful hands have rubbed one bronze toe to a shine. Smoke, shadow, and the weight of metal.

hook: Hanoi's oldest pagoda on a teardrop islet, a red stupa rising straight from West Lake.
facts: Traditionally founded in the 6th century, moved to this West Lake islet around 1615
An eleven-tier red stupa was added in 2003
Grows a bodhi tree grown from a cutting of the tree at Bodh Gaya in India
w: The city's oldest pagoda began by a river, then fled the eroding bank for a teardrop islet in West Lake around 1615. A slim red stupa now climbs eleven tiers from the water, and a bodhi tree grown from a cutting of the Buddha's own shades the yard. Best at sunset, with the lake gone gold.

hook: A bronze-casters' pagoda by Truc Bach Lake, home to a colossal cast Buddha.
facts: Ngu Xa was settled by bronze-casters who moved to the capital around the 16th century
The pagoda holds a seated Amitabha Buddha about four metres tall, cast 1949 to 1952
Only one family bronze workshop is said to remain in the old craft quarter
w: In the old bronze-casters' quarter by Truc Bach Lake sits the proof of their craft: a seated Amitabha Buddha, some four metres of bronze poured between 1949 and 1952 on a hundred-petal lotus. The foundries that filled these lanes have dwindled to one stubborn family. Metal as devotion.

hook: A dim 1950s coffee house hung with paintings traded by artists who couldn't pay their tab.
facts: Founded by Mr Lam, who set up at 60 Nguyen Huu Huan in the 1950s
Became a haunt of painters including Bui Xuan Phai in the late colonial years
Some artists settled their bills in canvases, building an accidental gallery
w: Painters drifted to this dim coffee house in the 1950s, among them Bui Xuan Phai, who immortalised the city's crumbling streets. Short of cash, some settled their tabs in canvases, and the walls slowly filled with real art. Connoisseurs skip the museum and drink here instead. Coffee paid in paint.

hook: A concrete air-raid shelter rediscovered beneath a grand hotel, where Joan Baez once sheltered.
facts: Bomb shelter under the Metropole, lost for decades, rediscovered during 2011 works
Six small rooms with ventilation for around 40 guests during the bombing raids
Joan Baez sheltered here in 1972 and recorded part of a song amid the bombs
w: Workers digging by the bar in 2011 broke into a forgotten concrete warren under the grand old Metropole, a bomb shelter where guests once waited out air raids. Joan Baez sheltered here in 1972 and sang into the dark as the bombs fell. Damp, low-ceilinged, and quietly extraordinary.

hook: A tiny upstairs guild shrine to silk traders, hidden above the shops of the old silk street.
facts: Communal house of the silk-collar guild, on Hang Dao, the old silk street
Origins traced to the 17th-century Le dynasty, rebuilt over two floors in 1941
Worships guardian spirits including Bach Ma; restored with the French city of Toulouse
w: Above the clamour of the old silk street hides a small communal house where the silk-collar guild once honoured its guardian spirits. Founded under the Le lords, rebuilt over two floors in 1941, it keeps a stone stele and the hush of a trade's patron shrine. Climb past the shopfronts to find it.

n: Hai Ba Trung Temple, Dong Nhan
hook: A temple to two rebel queens, holding red-faced statues said to have floated up the river.
facts: Honours the Trung sisters, who led a 1st-century revolt against Chinese rule
Founded in 1142 and later moved to the Dong Nhan riverbank site
Designated a special national heritage site in 2019
legend: Legend says the sisters' spirits turned to stone statues that floated down the river to this beach
w: Two sisters raised a revolt against China in the year 40 and drowned themselves rather than surrender. This temple honours them with red-faced statues, arms raised as if mid-dive. Legend says their spirits hardened into stone and floated up the river to this very beach. Defiance, enshrined.
